name: Jikininki
description: Jikininki, literally 'human-eating ghosts,' are cursed spirits in Japanese folklore that feast upon human corpses. They are often depicted as the spirits of greedy, selfish, or impious individuals, particularly priests, who abused their positions or hoarded wealth during their lifetimes. As punishment for their transgressions, they are reborn as grotesque creatures condemned to consume decaying flesh for eternity. Their appearance is often monstrous, with decaying skin, sharp claws, and glowing eyes, reflecting their corrupted souls and gruesome diet. They are said to roam graveyards and desolate areas, seeking out freshly buried bodies to satisfy their insatiable hunger. Some stories suggest that Jikininki retain a semblance of their former selves, experiencing shame and remorse for their actions, while others portray them as purely driven by their monstrous hunger, devoid of any human emotion. Encounters with Jikininki are considered extremely dangerous, as they are driven by their primal need to consume human flesh and are unlikely to show mercy to the living. Their existence serves as a cautionary tale about the consequences of greed and the importance of living a virtuous life.
subtitle: Human-eating ghosts cursed to feast.
appearance: Jikininki are typically described as having a gaunt, emaciated appearance with decaying skin, often a grayish or greenish hue. Their eyes glow with an eerie light, and their teeth are sharp and elongated, adapted for tearing flesh. They possess long, sharp claws, and their bodies are often covered in dirt and grime from their grave-robbing activities. Their forms are grotesque and unsettling, reflecting their corrupted nature and gruesome diet. Some accounts depict them as retaining vestiges of their former human appearance, while others portray them as almost entirely monstrous.
habitat: Jikininki primarily inhabit graveyards, cemeteries, and other desolate areas where corpses are buried. They are also said to roam remote villages and abandoned temples, searching for opportunities to feed. They prefer areas with little or no human presence, allowing them to carry out their gruesome activities undisturbed.
behavior: Jikininki are driven by an insatiable hunger for human flesh and are constantly searching for new sources of sustenance. They are solitary creatures, avoiding contact with other Jikininki and humans alike. They are stealthy and cunning, using their ability to blend into the darkness to ambush their prey. While some stories suggest that Jikininki retain a degree of their former human consciousness and experience remorse, most portray them as purely driven by their monstrous hunger, devoid of empathy or compassion.

keyMyths.title: The Priest and the Jikininki
keyMyths.description: A Buddhist priest encounters a Jikininki who reveals its past life as a greedy priest punished for his avarice. The encounter serves as a lesson about the importance of compassion and the dangers of greed.

sources.author: Lafcadio Hearn
sources.work: Kwaidan: Stories and Studies of Strange Things
sources.description: A collection of Japanese ghost stories, including tales of Jikininki.
sources.text: A collection of Japanese ghost stories, including tales of Jikininki.
